risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used computer-generated randomization to assign new reviewers to either the intervention or control group." Comment: This explicitly states a proper random method was used, so the allocation sequence was truly random and this domain is at low risk of bias.

Summary: The report states that "We used computer-generated randomization to assign new reviewers to either the intervention or control group," but it does not describe any mechanism used to conceal the upcoming assignment from the person enrolling participants. Computer-generated randomization addresses sequence generation, not allocation concealment. Although it says editors invited reviewers "without knowledge of which new reviewers were assigned to the mentorship or control groups," this refers to post-randomization blinding of editors rather than concealment from the recruiter at enrollment. Comment: No explicit central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other clearly concealed assignment method is described.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those in the control group received no initial study-specific contacts and no intervention beyond standard journal practices... Over the course of the study editors invited reviewers in their standard fashion, without knowledge of which new reviewers were assigned to the mentorship or control groups." Comment: Participants in the intervention arm were explicitly contacted and told they were in a mentoring program, while the control group received no study-specific contact. This means participants/personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded to allocation. Because the outcomes were reviewer quality scores and mentoring-related behavior/satisfaction, knowledge of group assignment could plausibly influence performance and thus the outcomes, creating performance bias.

Quote: "All reviews at Annals are routinely rated by editors for quality on a previously reported 5-point scale" and "Our main outcome measures were the mean review quality rating score for each reviewer, and the slope in change ... in editor-assigned reviewer quality ratings ... These were calculated from review scores recorded contemporaneously within our editorial management software." Comment: The outcome assessor was the editor (or editors) rating review quality from journal records, so this is not patient self-report. However, the report does not explicitly state that the editor(s) who assigned the quality ratings were blinded to reviewer allocation, and the outcome is judgment-dependent/subjective (editor-assigned quality scale). Because knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence these ratings, but blinding is not reported, the risk of detection bias is unclear.

Quote: "Four subjects randomized to the mentorship group never made contact of any kind with their mentors, and thus did not receive the intended intervention. We therefore present below the per-protocol analysis excluding these four subjects; however we also performed a corresponding intention-to-treat analysis that yielded essentially identical results (data not shown)." Comment: Attrition was small (4 participants), limited to the intervention arm, and the authors report an ITT analysis with essentially identical results. This makes serious attrition bias unlikely despite the per-protocol primary presentation.
